error.signature: AI misuses Japanese keigo (敬語) levels in professional communication

verdict.summary: Japanese has three keigo levels (尊敬語, 謙譲語, 丁寧語). Mixing them incorrectly signals incompetence or disrespect. AI must select the right level for the context.

dead_ends.action: Use 尊敬語 (sonkeigo/respectful) when referring to own actions
dead_ends.why_fails: 尊敬語 elevates the subject. Using it for yourself (e.g., おっしゃる instead of 申す) sounds arrogant. Self-reference requires 謙譲語 (kenjougo/humble).
dead_ends.condition: AI speaking about its own actions in Japanese

dead_ends.action: Default to casual form (タメ口) for efficiency
dead_ends.why_fails: Casual speech in business context is a career-ending mistake in Japan. Even between same-age colleagues, business settings require です/ます form at minimum.

dead_ends.action: Apply double-keigo (二重敬語) thinking more polite is better
dead_ends.why_fails: Stacking honorifics (e.g., お読みになられる) is grammatically incorrect and sounds uneducated. One honorific form per verb is the rule.
dead_ends.condition: Trying to be extra polite in Japanese

workarounds.action: Use 丁寧語 (desu/masu) as safe default for general communication. Upgrade to 尊敬語 for clients/superiors, and 謙譲語 for own actions
For business emails, follow the standard structure: 拝啓 → seasonal greeting → 本文 → 敬具. Use お忙しいところ恐れ入りますが as a buffer phrase
When uncertain about keigo level, err on the side of formality. Being too polite is forgiven; being too casual is not.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Korean and Japanese honorific systems share structural similarities but have different rules
